cure.ref: Hank Shaw (Honest-Food) Cacciatore Salami + meatsandsausages fermented sausage
cure.note: Cure #2 (6.25% nitrite + 4% nitrate) at 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm ingoing nitrite plus a nitrate reservoir for the 2-4 week hang. CRITICAL GAP: current cure=None on a raw room-temp air-dried ground salami = direct botulism/Listeria risk. Long dry-cure REQUIRES Cure #2, not Cure #1.

process.note: Ferment ~24-48 h at 20-24C, 85-90% RH with starter culture (dextrose feed) to pH <=5.3; dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for ~2-4 weeks (32-38mm hog casing) to 30-35% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls, no thermal floor: nitrite (Cure #2) + pH drop to <=5.3 from fermentation + aw reduction via drying to 30-35% loss. Not cooked.

flag: SAFETY GAP: cure was None on a raw dry-fermented salami — set Cure #2 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm).
rationale: Small Italian dry salami; missing nitrite corrected to Cure #2 156 ppm and salt raised to 2.8% for aw control.

cure.ref: 2 Guys & A Cooler / Wikipedia Droewors
cure.note: Recommend Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm. Traditional droewors is nitrite-free (salt + vinegar + coriander, thin fast dry) and can be shelf-stable, but current cure=1 g/kg = only ~62 ppm is a half-measure that neither follows tradition nor gives full nitrite protection for a warm-dried ground sausage. For a safety-first app, Cure #2 at 156 ppm (nitrate reservoir for the warm 3-5 day dry) is the conservative call.

process.note: No fermentation culture; thin coils hung in warm moving air ~21-27C, 50-60% RH for 3-5 days to a dry, snappable stick (~40-50% weight loss). Vinegar assists acidification.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite + salt + vinegar (low pH) + rapid drying of thin coils to low aw. No thermal pasteurization step.

flag: SAFETY GAP: cure was 1 g/kg (~62 ppm) — below standard; recommend Cure #2 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm).
rationale: Warm-air-dried ground sausage; bumped sub-standard 62 ppm cure to full Cure #2 156 ppm and salt to 2.8%.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages Lap Cheong
cure.note: Recommend Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm (nitrate reservoir for the multi-day warm dry). Current cure=1 g/kg = ~62 ppm is below standard. Note lap cheong is a sweet sausage normally steamed/fried before eating, which lowers residual risk, but it is still air-dried warm for days as a raw product, so full nitrite protection is warranted.

process.note: No starter culture; high sugar + rice-wine. Dried warm ~45-50C for ~48 h (do not exceed 50C) or hung 3+ days at 15-20C, 60-70% RH to firm/translucent (~desired weight loss). Cooked before consumption.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + high sugar + salt lowering aw + drying; plus the product is cooked before eating. No pre-storage thermal pasteurization.

flag: SAFETY GAP: cure was 1 g/kg (~62 ppm) — below standard; recommend Cure #2 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm).
rationale: Sweet Chinese dried sausage; raised sub-standard 62 ppm cure to full Cure #2 156 ppm; salt kept ~2.5% (sugar aids aw).

cure.ref: meatsandsausages spreadable fermented sausage
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. CRITICAL GAP: current cure=None on a raw, fermented, weeks-aged spreadable Calabrian salami = botulism/Listeria risk. Nduja ferments and dries several weeks, so Cure #2 (nitrate reservoir) is required, not Cure #1.

process.note: High-chili soft paste stuffed in casing; ferment ~24-48 h at 20-24C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3, then dry/smoke-cure ~3-6 weeks at 12-15C, 75-85% RH. Stays spreadable but loses ~25-30%.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + fermentation pH drop + drying to reduced aw. High capsaicin/chili also inhibitory. Not cooked.

flag: SAFETY GAP: cure was None on a raw fermented aged spreadable salami — set Cure #2 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm).
rationale: Weeks-aged raw fermented paste; missing nitrite corrected to Cure #2 156 ppm, salt 2.6%.

cure.ref: UMAi Dry French Dry Sausage / meatsandsausages fermented sausage
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite plus nitrate reservoir. CRITICAL GAP: current cure=None on a slow-fermented French dry salami aged 3-6 weeks = botulism/Listeria risk. Long dry-cure REQUIRES Cure #2.

process.note: Mild slow ferment ~24-48 h at 18-22C, 85-90% RH with culture (or traditional slow acidification); dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for 3-6 weeks to 30-35% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + pH drop + drying to 30-35% loss lowering aw. Not cooked.

flag: SAFETY GAP: cure was None on a slow-fermented dry salami — set Cure #2 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm).
rationale: Classic French dry salami; missing nitrite corrected to Cure #2 156 ppm, salt 2.8%.

cure.ref: UMAi Dry Soppressata / meatsandsausages fermented sausage
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. CRITICAL GAP: current cure=None on a raw Italian dry-cured salami aged 4-8 weeks = botulism/Listeria risk. Long dry-cure REQUIRES Cure #2.

process.note: Coarse-ground, pressed; ferment ~24-48 h at 20-24C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for 4-8 weeks (larger casing) to 30-40% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + fermentation pH drop + drying to 30-40% loss lowering aw. Not cooked.

flag: SAFETY GAP: cure was None on a raw dry-cured salami — set Cure #2 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm).
rationale: Pressed Italian dry salami; missing nitrite corrected to Cure #2 156 ppm, salt 2.8%.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages Sucuk / Taste of Artisan dry-cured sujuk
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir (Cure #2 recommended when aging 30+ days, which the dry version does). CRITICAL GAP: current cure=None on a raw fermented air-dried Turkish sausage = botulism/Listeria risk. Requires Cure #2 for the dry style.

process.ref: Taste of Artisan / Turkish Food Codex pH
process.note: Ferment ~48 h at ~24C, 80% RH with culture to pH ~5.2-5.4; then dry/rest 5+ days at 10-16C, 65-75% RH, continuing 2-4 weeks to ~30-40% weight loss (pressed flat).

safe.ref: Turkish Food Codex / Taste of Artisan
safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + fermentation to pH 5.2-5.4 + drying to reduced aw. Garlic/spices additionally inhibitory. Not cooked.

flag: SAFETY GAP: cure was None on a raw fermented dry sausage — set Cure #2 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm).
rationale: Turkish dry-fermented beef sausage; missing nitrite corrected to Cure #2 156 ppm, salt 2.8%.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages Spanish Chorizo (dry)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct as-is — dry-cured Spanish chorizo air-dries 4-8 weeks and needs the nitrate reservoir. Pimenton provides color/flavor, not preservation.

process.note: Hold/ferment ~48 h at 22-24C, 85-90% RH; dry 12-16C, RH stepping 85->75% for 4-8 weeks to ~30% weight loss. Any smoke is cold (<22C).

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + pH drop + drying to reduced aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Well-supported: Cure #2 156 ppm kept; salt held at 2.5% within dry-cure range.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages fermented sausage (Hungarian paprika dry sausage)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — Csabai is a smoked, paprika-rich Hungarian dry-fermented sausage aged several weeks, so Cure #2 is right (cold smoke only).

process.note: Ferment ~48 h at 18-22C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; cold-smoke <22C optional; dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for 3-5 weeks to ~30-35% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + fermentation pH drop + drying to reduced aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Hungarian smoked dry salami; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt raised slightly to 2.5% for aw.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages fermented sausage (Tuscan fennel salami)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — Tuscan fennel salami dries 6-10 weeks (medium/large casing), needs Cure #2.

process.note: Mild ferment ~24-48 h at 20-24C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for 6-10 weeks to 30-40% weight loss. Fennel seed for flavor.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + pH drop + drying to 30-40% loss lowering aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Tuscan fennel salami; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt 2.6% for aw control.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages fermented sausage (Catalan fuet)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — thin Catalan dry salami, fast but still 2-4 week hang with surface mold; Cure #2 appropriate.

process.note: Ferment ~24-48 h at 20-24C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; dry 12-15C, 80-85% RH for 2-4 weeks (thin 34-40mm) to ~30-35% loss; white Penicillium bloom typical.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + pH drop + rapid drying of thin casing lowering aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Thin Catalan dry salami; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt 2.6%.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages Kabanosy / fermented sausage
cure.note: For this DRY/fermented catalog entry, Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm is appropriate for the multi-week hang. (Note: Marianski's classic kabanosy is actually cooked to 68-71C and would use Cure #1; a truly raw air-dried version needs Cure #2. Confirm which the app intends.) Cure #2 2.5 kept.

process.note: Thin snack sticks. Dry version: ferment/hold with culture then dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH ~1-2 weeks to ~40% weight loss (55% yield). Cold smoke optional.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls (raw dry version): nitrite (Cure #2) + drying of thin sticks to low aw (~40% loss). If cooked variant, add 71C thermal step instead.

flag: Salt was 1.8% (18 g/kg) — below the ~2.7% aw target for a dry product; raised to 2.5%.
rationale: Kept Cure #2 156 ppm; raised low salt from 18 to 25 g/kg for aw control; flagged the cook-vs-dry ambiguity in cure note.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages fermented / semi-dry sausage
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir for the semi-dry (podsuszana) hang. Correct for the air-dried version. (Traditional Krakowska is often cooked+lightly dried, which would take Cure #1; the 'podsuszana' dried style justifies Cure #2.)

process.note: Large-diameter; cure meat, stuff, cold-smoke, then dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH ~2-3 weeks to ~25-30% weight loss (semi-dry).

safe.note: Dry/semi-dry controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + drying to reduced aw; if a cooked variant is used add a 71C thermal step.

flag: Salt was 2.0% (20 g/kg) — low for a dry product; raised to 2.5%.
rationale: Semi-dry Polish sausage; Cure #2 156 ppm kept; raised salt 20->25 g/kg for aw.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages fermented sausage (Balkan paprika dry sausage)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — Kulen is a spicy Croatian/Serbian smoked dry-fermented sausage aged 2-4 months, needs Cure #2.

process.note: Ferment ~48 h at 18-22C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; cold-smoke <22C; dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for 8-16 weeks (large casing) to 35-40% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + fermentation pH drop + long dry to 35-40% loss lowering aw. Paprika/chili additionally inhibitory. Not cooked.

rationale: Balkan spicy dry salami; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt raised to 2.5% for aw.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages Landjager
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — pressed, fermented, air-dried semi-dry sausage; Cure #2 for the drying window.

process.note: Ferment ~72 h at 20C, 90-95% RH with culture; pressed flat in molds; cold-smoke <20C; dry ~3 weeks at 12-14C, 80-85% RH to ~30% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + fermentation pH drop + drying to ~30% loss lowering aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Pressed fermented dried sausage; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt 2.6%.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages fermented sausage (Salame Milano)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — fine-ground Italian dry salami aged 6-10 weeks, needs Cure #2.

process.note: Fine grind (rice-grain fat); ferment ~24-48 h at 20-24C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for 6-10 weeks to 30-40% weight loss; Penicillium bloom.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + pH drop + long dry to 30-40% loss lowering aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Fine Italian dry salami; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt 2.6%.

cure.ref: Nem Chua safety literature / Wikipedia; meatsandsausages curing
cure.note: Recommend Cure #1 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite (NOT Cure #2). GAP: current cure=None on a raw, room-temperature fermented pork eaten uncooked = botulism/Listeria/parasite risk. This is a SHORT-cycle (1-3 day) fresh ferment, so nitrate has no time to reduce — Cure #1 is the technically correct nitrite source; many modern/commercial recipes already add nitrite/nitrate. Traditional versions rely only on rapid strong acidification, which is not reliably safe for an app audience.

process.ref: Nem Chua production hygiene study / eathealthy365
process.note: Rapid lactic fermentation at ambient ~28-30C for 1-3 days to a sharp sour pH (<4.6) via native/added LAB and sugar; wrapped in leaves, eaten fresh (not dried to 30-40% loss). Keep refrigerated after souring; not shelf-stable like dry salami.

safe.note: Controls: nitrite (Cure #1) + fast, strong pH drop below ~4.6 from lactic fermentation (LAB also produce a bacteriocin). NOT aw-controlled (undried) and NOT cooked — safety hinges on rapid acidification; higher inherent risk than dry salami.

flag: SAFETY GAP: cure was None on a raw uncooked fermented pork — set Cure #1 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm); short cycle means Cure #1, not #2.
rationale: Fresh Vietnamese sour pork; added nitrite (Cure #1 156 ppm) for the short ferment; salt kept ~2.2% (this is fresh, not dried).

cure.ref: meatsandsausages Pepperoni (dry)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — slow-fermented dry pepperoni air-dries 6-8 weeks, needs Cure #2.

process.note: Ferment ~72 h at 20C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; optional cold smoke <22C; dry 12-16C, 80-85% RH ~6-8 weeks to ~30% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + fermentation pH drop + drying to reduced aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Slow-fermented dry pepperoni; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt 2.5%.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages fermented sausage (Spanish salchichon)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — peppercorn-studded Spanish dry salami aged 6-10 weeks, needs Cure #2.

process.note: Ferment ~24-48 h at 20-24C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for 6-10 weeks to 30-40% weight loss; black pepper for flavor.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + pH drop + long dry lowering aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Spanish peppercorn dry salami; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt 2.6%.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages fermented sausage (Serbian smoked dry sausage)
cure.note: Cure #2 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite + nitrate reservoir. Correct — Sremska is a smoked, paprika-spiced Serbian dry-fermented sausage aged several weeks, needs Cure #2 (cold smoke).

process.note: Ferment ~48 h at 18-22C, 85-90% RH with culture to pH <=5.3; cold-smoke <22C; dry 12-15C, 75-80% RH for 3-5 weeks to ~30-35% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + fermentation pH drop + drying to reduced aw. Not cooked.

rationale: Serbian smoked dry sausage; Cure #2 156 ppm confirmed; salt raised to 2.5% for aw.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages spreadable fermented sausage
cure.note: Recommend Cure #1 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite, NOT Cure #2. Marianski is explicit: for short-cycle spreadable raw sausages the production is too brief for curing bacteria to reduce nitrate, so Cure #2's nitrate is wasted/inappropriate — use Cure #1. Current cure=2.5 (amount fine) but ensure it is Cure #1 type.

process.note: Fine raw paste; brief ferment ~24 h at room temp, ~80% RH with fast starter culture (pH ~4.7-5.1); light cold smoke. Stays soft/spreadable, NOT dried to 30-40% loss; keep refrigerated (short shelf life).

safe.note: Controls: nitrite (Cure #1) + salt/sugar lowering aw ~0.2 units + fast starter culture out-competing pathogens + brief pH drop. Neither cooked nor fully dried, so must be refrigerated; higher risk than dry salami.

flag: Cure TYPE correction: this short-cycle spreadable should use Cure #1 (nitrite), not Cure #2 — nitrate cannot convert in time.
rationale: Raw spreadable smoked sausage; amount kept 2.5 g/kg (156 ppm) but switched type to Cure #1 per Marianski for the short cycle; salt 2.5%.

cure.ref: 2 Guys & A Cooler Bresaola / Gastrochemist whole-muscle basics
cure.note: Cure #2 recommended for this long (4-8 week) whole-muscle beef dry-cure. Standard pink-salt dose is 0.25% of meat = 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm ingoing nitrite (EQ method). Current cure=2 g/kg (~125 ppm) is below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard — raised to 2.5 g/kg. Method: Cure #2 by EQ %, applied with the salt.

process.ref: 2 Guys & A Cooler / EatCuredMeat equilibrium curing
process.note: EQ-cure whole eye-of-round in fridge (~3C) ~2-3 weeks (7 days per 1 inch radius), turning; then dry 12-13C, 70-75% RH for 4-8 weeks to ~30-40% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + salt-driven aw reduction (intact muscle, no fermentation) + drying to 30-40% loss. Not cooked; whole-muscle lower interior risk than ground.

flag: SAFETY GAP (marginal): cure was 2 g/kg (~125 ppm) — below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard; raised to 2.5 g/kg.
rationale: Whole-muscle beef dry-cure; standardized Cure #2 to 0.25% EQ (156 ppm); salt 3% typical for bresaola.

cure.ref: Gastrochemist whole-muscle basics / EatCuredMeat EQ curing
cure.note: Cure #2 for the long (6-12 week) pork neck dry-cure. Standard 0.25% pink salt = 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm ingoing nitrite (EQ). Current cure=2 g/kg (~125 ppm) below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard — raised to 2.5. Method: Cure #2 by EQ % with the salt.

process.note: EQ-cure pork coppa muscle in fridge ~2-3 weeks turning; case in bung; dry 12-13C, 70-75% RH for 6-12 weeks to ~30-40% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + salt-driven aw reduction (intact muscle) + long drying to 30-40% loss. Not cooked.

flag: SAFETY GAP (marginal): cure was 2 g/kg (~125 ppm) — below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard; raised to 2.5 g/kg.
rationale: Whole-muscle pork neck dry-cure; standardized Cure #2 to 0.25% EQ (156 ppm); salt 3%.

cure.ref: Gastrochemist whole-muscle basics / EatCuredMeat EQ curing
cure.note: Cure #2 for the 3-6 week pork-jowl dry-cure. Standard 0.25% pink salt = 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm ingoing nitrite (EQ). Current cure=2 g/kg (~125 ppm) below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard — raised to 2.5. (Guanciale is normally rendered/cooked before eating, lowering residual risk, but nitrite is still warranted for the raw cure.) Method: Cure #2 by EQ %.

process.note: EQ dry-cure pork jowl in fridge ~1-2 weeks turning; then hang 12-13C, 70-75% RH for ~3-6 weeks to ~30% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + salt-driven aw reduction (intact fatty muscle) + drying; typically cooked before eating. Not thermally pasteurized during cure.

flag: SAFETY GAP (marginal): cure was 2 g/kg (~125 ppm) — below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard; raised to 2.5 g/kg.
rationale: Whole-muscle pork jowl dry-cure; standardized Cure #2 to 0.25% EQ (156 ppm); salt 3%.

cure.ref: Gastrochemist whole-muscle basics / EatCuredMeat EQ curing
cure.note: Cure #2 for the 4-8 week pork-loin dry-cure. Standard 0.25% pink salt = 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm ingoing nitrite (EQ). Current cure=2 g/kg (~125 ppm) below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard — raised to 2.5. Method: Cure #2 by EQ %.

process.note: EQ-cure pork loin in fridge ~1-2 weeks turning; case; dry 12-13C, 70-75% RH for 4-8 weeks to ~30-40% weight loss (lean loin dries faster/case-hardens if RH low).

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + salt-driven aw reduction (intact lean muscle) + drying to 30-40% loss. Not cooked.

flag: SAFETY GAP (marginal): cure was 2 g/kg (~125 ppm) — below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard; raised to 2.5 g/kg.
rationale: Whole-muscle pork loin dry-cure; standardized Cure #2 to 0.25% EQ (156 ppm); salt 3%.

cure.ref: Gastrochemist whole-muscle basics / EatCuredMeat EQ curing
cure.note: Cure #2 for the 2-4 week pork-belly dry-cure. Standard 0.25% pink salt = 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm ingoing nitrite (EQ). Current cure=2 g/kg (~125 ppm) below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard — raised to 2.5. (Pancetta is usually cooked before eating; nitrite still needed for the raw cure.) Method: Cure #2 by EQ %.

process.note: EQ dry-cure pork belly in fridge ~1 week per 25mm thickness turning; rolled (arrotolata) or flat (tesa); dry 12-13C, 70-75% RH for 2-4 weeks to ~25-30% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + salt-driven aw reduction (intact belly) + drying; usually cooked before eating. Not pasteurized during cure.

flag: SAFETY GAP (marginal): cure was 2 g/kg (~125 ppm) — below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard; raised to 2.5 g/kg.
rationale: Whole-muscle pork belly dry-cure; standardized Cure #2 to 0.25% EQ (156 ppm); salt 3%.

cure.ref: Gastrochemist whole-muscle basics / EatCuredMeat EQ curing
cure.note: Cure #2 for the multi-week cold-smoked pork-leg dry-cure. Standard 0.25% pink salt = 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm ingoing nitrite (EQ). Current cure=2 g/kg (~125 ppm) below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard — raised to 2.5. Method: Cure #2 by EQ %, with cold smoke.

process.note: EQ dry-cure deboned pork leg in fridge ~2-3 weeks turning; repeated light COLD smoke (<20C) over days; then dry 12-14C, 70-75% RH for several weeks to months to ~30-35% weight loss.

safe.note: Dry-cure controls: nitrite (Cure #2) + salt-driven aw reduction (intact muscle) + cold smoke + long drying to 30-35% loss. Not cooked.

flag: SAFETY GAP (marginal): cure was 2 g/kg (~125 ppm) — below the 0.25%/156 ppm standard; raised to 2.5 g/kg.
rationale: Whole-muscle cold-smoked pork-leg dry-cure; standardized Cure #2 to 0.25% EQ (156 ppm); salt 3%.
